headline: She Started Law School at 47. Now She Runs It.
meta_description: A woman overcame age-related doubts to attend law school in her 40s and eventually founded her own all-women firm.
summary_short: Dismissing her own doubts about being 'too old,' a woman pursued law school at 47 and built something remarkable.
body:
Age became the first barrier—not from others, but from within. A woman in her late 40s questioned whether starting law school was foolish, whether she'd somehow missed her window. The self-doubt was real. The risk was real too.

But she enrolled anyway.

Over a decade later, that decision has compounded into something far larger than a single law degree. She now leads her own all-women law firm, proof that the timeline society suggests—finish school by 25, establish yourself by 35—is merely one possibility among many.

Her journey reveals what happens when someone chooses to act despite their fear of being the oldest person in the room. It's not about pretending age doesn't matter. It's about recognizing that starting late often means starting with clarity, resilience, and purpose that younger versions of ourselves might lack.
